name: Hector
description: Hector, son of King Priam and Queen Hecuba of Troy, stands as a paragon of Trojan heroism in Greek mythology, particularly within Homer's *Iliad*. He is not a god, but rather a mortal hero elevated through his actions and character. As the leader of the Trojan army, Hector embodies martial prowess, strategic acumen, and unwavering devotion to his city and family. Unlike many figures in the epic who are driven by personal ambition or divine intervention, Hector's motivations are rooted in a profound sense of duty and responsibility. He is depicted as a loving husband to Andromache and a doting father to Astyanax, showcasing a tender side often juxtaposed with his battlefield ferocity. His tragic death at the hands of Achilles marks a pivotal moment in the Trojan War, underscoring the futility of war and the inevitability of fate. Hector's legacy transcends mere military achievement; he represents the virtues of courage, honor, and self-sacrifice in the face of overwhelming odds, solidifying his place as one of the most revered figures in Greek literature.
subtitle: Defender of Troy

keyMyths.title: Hector's Farewell to Andromache
keyMyths.description: Before returning to battle, Hector visits his wife Andromache and son Astyanax. He knows his fate may be sealed, yet he comforts Andromache, urging her to accept her destiny and maintain honor. This scene highlights Hector's humanity, revealing his love and concern for his family amidst the chaos of war. It also foreshadows the tragic future awaiting Troy and its royal family after his death. The poignancy of this farewell contributes to Hector's status as a sympathetic and relatable hero.

keyMyths.title: Hector's Duel with Ajax
keyMyths.description: During a lull in the fighting, Hector challenges a Greek warrior to single combat. Ajax, known for his immense strength, steps forward. The two heroes battle fiercely, but neither can gain a decisive advantage. The duel ends in a draw, and the warriors exchange gifts as a sign of respect. This encounter demonstrates Hector's courage and skill, as well as the mutual respect that can exist even between enemies in wartime. It also reveals the limitations of even the greatest heroes.

keyMyths.title: Hector's Death at the Hands of Achilles
keyMyths.description: Driven by rage over the death of Patroclus, Achilles returns to battle and seeks out Hector. Despite warnings from his parents, Hector stands his ground outside the walls of Troy. After a desperate chase around the city, Hector faces Achilles in single combat. He is ultimately slain, and Achilles desecrates his body by dragging it behind his chariot. This event marks a turning point in the Trojan War, signaling the imminent fall of Troy and cementing Hector's status as a tragic hero who died defending his city.
